results.title: Mechanics in Magic: Retrace | Article by Mark Wischkaemper
results.content:
Also, there are a couple of important rulings about Retrace that matter. When you cast a card with Retrace from your Graveyard, you're casting it from the Graveyard, not your Hand (so Phage, the Untouchable would kill you immediately, for example). You must pay all associated costs to cast the card and discard a Land card to cast a spell with Retrace. If a spell with Retrace is countered, it still goes back to the Graveyard... where it may be cast with Retrace again. And finally, if you cast an Instant or Sorcery with Retrace from your Hand and it resolves, it returns to your Graveyard and you may cast it again immediately for its Retrace cost before anyone may remove it from your Graveyard. You retain priority, so you can cast it right then no problem. Note this is only true with [...] ## Talk more about this

Retrace is a keyword ability put on spells starting during Eventide. The only spells which have it originally are Instants and Sorceries, though other cards later give it to other card types (we'll get there).

The gist is this: you may cast a card with Retrace from your Graveyard by paying its costs, acknowledging all timing restrictions (you can't cast a Sorcery during someone else's turn, for example), and discarding a Land card in addition to all other costs. As long as you can meet those requirements, you can cast the spell again. [...] # CoolStuffInc.com

# Mechanics in Magic: Retrace

Mark Wischkaemper

By Mark Wischkaemper

Posted on April 29, 2025

Reddit

markwischkaemper@gmail.com

Ever played a game of Magic where it feels like the other player is doing the same thing over and over, and none of it is nice to you? Retrace is the kind of mechanic that makes games feel like that - which is why it's been rated a 7 of 10 on the Storm Scale.

# 10 Most Common Customer Complaints and Their Solutions

No matter the industry, customer complaints are a reality of doing business. Complaints highlight areas where expectations aren’t being met, whether a delayed response, a billing error, or a frustrating return policy.

This is especially true in high-touch industries like banking, healthcare, and retail, where customer interactions are frequent and service quality matters most.

While complaints can feel like setbacks, they’re actually valuable insights. They reveal gaps in service, operational inefficiencies, and opportunities for improvement. [...] ### 5. Repetitive Information Requests

Customers often find it frustrating to repeatedly provide the same personal information, account details, or case history. This is especially common in healthcare, banking, and customer support interactions, where inefficient data systems lead to unnecessary repetition.

To solve this issue, businesses can integrate self-check-in kiosks and CRM systems that allow customers to pre-fill or verify their details once, eliminating the need to repeat information.

For example, many healthcare facilities now use kiosks where patients can scan their IDs to check in rather than filling out paper forms multiple times. This speeds up the process, reduces frustration, and enhances the customer experience. [...] ## Why Listening to Customer Complaints Matters

Customer complaints are more than just frustrations. They provide valuable insights into service gaps, friction points, and unmet expectations. Yet, many businesses fail to recognize their importance.

Research shows that 96% of unhappy customers don’t complain, but 91% of them will leave without saying a word. This means businesses often lose customers without knowing why.

Frequent customer complaints act as early warning signals, helping businesses identify issues before they escalate.

When handled correctly, they can reduce churn, improve customer satisfaction, and strengthen brand loyalty. A well-resolved complaint can turn a frustrated customer into a long-term advocate.

results.content: removal can be tricky to use correctly to get it out of there. [...] Retrace appears on Instants and Sorceries, and is a mechanic that lets you get a lot of use out of those spells. If a card with Retrace is in your graveyard, you may pay its mana cost and discard a land from your hand to cast it from there, still obeying all normal timing restrictions. However, unlike a lot of other mechanics that let you cast cards from your graveyard (like Flashback), the card goes right back into your graveyard, and you can just keep Retracing it – the only limit is how much you can pay the cost, including having a land in hand to discard. Even if the spell is countered when it’s being retraced, it still ends up back in the graveyard. And while it’s being cast, it’s on the stack, not in the yard, so even graveyard removal can be tricky to use correctly to get it out of

And in Legacy, the plus one and minus one together could easily turn Ren 6 into a land destruction engine that punished any deck playing X1s and that was too powerful for the format, resulting in it eventually getting banned there. Renin 6 is still legal in Modern and Vintage, though, where it's less busted because modern doesn't really have any good lands that destroy other lands, and vintage is a powerful enough format that it can keep up with Renin 6's nonsense. In those two formats, Renin 6 is [...] This video is sponsored by Card Kingdom. If you want Magic singles or sealed product, use my link in the description. [Music] In Magic the Gathering, retrace is a keyword ability that was originally introduced in 2008's Even Tide. If a card with retrace is in your graveyard, you can cast it from your graveyard by paying its cost and discarding a land from your hand. So unlike the similar and more widely used flashback, you do have to give something up in hand to cast it again. But Retrace does have one big advantage over Flashback and most other mechanics and effects let you cast spells from your graveyard because cards with Retrace don't get exiled when you cast them. In other words, you can cast a card like Savage Conception every single turn, provided you have a land to discard and the [...] bit of play in monor red aggro decks in block, but it caught on in a more significant way in both modern and legacy. In both formats, it was played alongside Life from the Lom, a powerful card that goes great with Retrace because it can load your graveyard with dredge and return lands from your graveyard when you cast it. Most recently, it's seen a bit of play in Popper, a format where only commons are legal, where it's been played in reanimator decks, and it might find more success in Popper in the future.
